category: NBFC collapse from asset-liability mismatch and an opaque group structure, triggering system-wide contagion
risk_signals: Long-gestation infrastructure assets funded largely with short-term borrowings, creating a severe asset-liability maturity mismatch
A sprawling group of subsidiaries and special-purpose vehicles that obscured the true consolidated leverage
High leverage sustained by continual roll-over of short-term debt in the commercial-paper market
Strong external credit ratings that masked the underlying fragility until defaults began
analyst_reasoning: Funding decades-long infrastructure projects with short-term paper works only as long as the paper can always be rolled over. An opaque web of subsidiaries hid how much leverage sat across the group, so lenders and rating agencies underpriced the risk. Because NBFCs are deeply interconnected with banks and mutual funds through the commercial-paper and debt markets, the failure of a large one transmits stress outward — as the RBI's Financial Stability Report warned that the failure of the largest NBFCs could cause losses comparable to a big bank.
investigation_steps: Build the consolidated funding maturity ladder across all group entities to size the roll-over cliff
Consolidate leverage across every subsidiary and SPV rather than trusting entity-level figures
Map interconnections to banks, mutual funds, and the commercial-paper market to gauge contagion channels
Stress-test the group against a loss of access to short-term wholesale funding
decision: After IL&FS group entities began defaulting on their obligations in 2018, the government superseded its board and the matter moved into insolvency resolution; the RBI subsequently tracked the resulting NBFC-sector stress and contagion in its Financial Stability Reports.
outcome: The IL&FS defaults froze confidence in the commercial-paper market, pressured mutual funds facing redemptions, and squeezed other NBFCs reliant on short-term wholesale funding — a system-wide contagion that validated the RBI's network model of NBFC interconnectedness.
lessons_learned: Funding long-term assets with short-term liabilities is a liquidity trap regardless of asset quality
Opaque group structures hide leverage; consolidation across all entities is essential to see the real risk
NBFCs are systemically interconnected, so a single large failure can transmit contagion to banks and mutual funds
Credit ratings can lag reality, so they are not a substitute for independent liquidity and leverage analysis
